action.content:
You are a field linguist. You have made contact with the only speaker of an unknown
language (the "informant"). Nobody has ever written down this language before, and you
have NO examples to start from. Your task is to work out how it works entirely on your
own, and then translate sentences into it correctly.

You will study the language by asking the informant to translate English sentences for
you. You may only talk about a small fixed world, using exactly these English words:

Nouns: bird, boat, hill, ship, river, lake, road, farmer, stone, king, dog, spear, tree, hunter
Adjectives: small, tall, dark, cold, big, red, blue, old
Verbs (write them in this base form): chase, find, hold, hear, feed, see, carry, watch
Articles: the (definite), a (indefinite)
Number words: two, three, four, five, six (no number word = one / singular)
These are ALL the words in play - the language has no other words.

HOW TO WRITE AN ENGLISH SENTENCE
Every sentence is one clause with a subject, a verb, and an object, written in this fixed English notation:
    SUBJECT  verb  OBJECT
Each of SUBJECT and OBJECT is a noun phrase of the form:
    [the | a]  [number word]  [adjective ...]  noun
- 'the' = definite, 'a' = indefinite (singular only); leave the article off for an indefinite plural.
- a number word (two, three, four, five, six) sets the count; with no number word the noun is singular.
- zero or more adjectives may come before the noun.
Examples of well-formed English sentences you may send:
    a bird chase a boat
    six birds chase a boat
    a small bird chase a boat

HOW THE SESSION WORKS
1. INVESTIGATION: You have 5 rounds of questioning. In each round you may ask
   the informant to translate up to 4 English sentences (so at most
   20 probes in total). The informant answers each one truthfully and
   exactly. You are starting from nothing, so spend your early probes establishing the
   basic words and word order, then test how number, case, definiteness, agreement and
   any sound changes work. Choose your probes deliberately - test specific hypotheses.
2. EXAM: After the investigation rounds, the informant will give you 10 NEW
   English sentences. You must translate each one INTO the language. Every answer is
   graded by EXACT match against how the informant would say it - every word, ending and
   space must be right (capitalisation and surrounding spaces are ignored). The exam
   sentences will use the same vocabulary but in combinations you have not been shown, so
   you must induce the general rules, not just memorise examples.

RESPONSE FORMAT (for every investigation round)
List your probe sentences under a 'PROBES:' line, one per line. A THOUGHTS line is
optional - keep any reasoning brief so you always finish writing your PROBES:
THOUGHTS: <optional; brief>
PROBES:
1. <an English sentence, written as described above>
2. <another English sentence>
(up to 4 lines; at least one)

Begin your first round of probes now.
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: I need to establish the basic word order (Subject-Verb-Object) and how the verb conjugates or remains invariant. I will start with simple S-V-O sentences using singular nouns and the verb "see" to see if the verb changes form based on the subject or object. I'll also test if the verb is always in the base form as hinted by the prompt's examples.

PROBES:
1. a bird see a boat
2. a boat see a bird
3. a bird see a small boat
4. a small boat see a bird
action.label: response

probes: a bird see a small boat
a small boat see a bird

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "a bird see a boat"  =>  vuma pato fovu
2. "a boat see a bird"  =>  pato vuma fovu
3. "a bird see a small boat"  =>  vuma finu pato fovu
4. "a small boat see a bird"  =>  finu pato vuma fovu
STATUS: investigation round 1 of 5 done. Probes used: 4 of 20. 4 investigation round(s) left before the exam (10 sentences).
Send your next probes now (a PROBES list; brief THOUGHTS optional).
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: The verb "vuma" remains invariant regardless of the subject or object, confirming it is a base form verb. The word order is strictly Subject-Verb-Object (SVO). Adjectives precede the noun within the subject phrase. Now I need to test the indefinite article "a" versus the definite article "the" and how they affect the word order or form. I will also test if the verb changes when the subject is plural (implied by no number word = singular, so I need to explicitly test plural "two birds").

PROBES:
1. a bird see the boat
2. the boat see a bird
3. two birds see a boat
4. a boat see two birds
action.label: response

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "a bird see the boat"  =>  vuma pato na fovu
2. "the boat see a bird"  =>  pato na vuma fovu
3. "two birds see a boat"  =>  vumavu nani pato fovu
4. "a boat see two birds"  =>  pato vumavu nani fovu
STATUS: investigation round 2 of 5 done. Probes used: 8 of 20. 3 investigation round(s) left before the exam (10 sentences).
Send your next probes now (a PROBES list; brief THOUGHTS optional).
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: The definite article "na" appears immediately after the noun in the object position ("pato na"), while the indefinite "a" is absent. The verb "vuma" is invariant. Plural nouns are formed by adding "-vu" to the singular stem ("birds" = "vumavu"). The word order remains SVO. Now I need to test the interaction between the definite article and the subject, and verify if the verb changes when the subject is plural (e.g., "two birds see..."). I will also test if the definite article can appear before the subject noun.

PROBES:
1. the bird see the boat
2. the boat see the bird
3. two birds see the boat
4. a boat see the two birds
action.label: response

probes: a boat see the two birds

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "the bird see the boat"  =>  vuma na pato na fovu
2. "the boat see the bird"  =>  pato na vuma na fovu
3. "two birds see the boat"  =>  vumavu nani pato na fovu
4. "a boat see the two birds"  =>  pato vumavu nani na fovu
STATUS: investigation round 3 of 5 done. Probes used: 12 of 20. 2 investigation round(s) left before the exam (10 sentences).
Send your next probes now (a PROBES list; brief THOUGHTS optional).
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: The definite article "na" appears after the noun in both subject and object positions ("pato na", "vuma na"). The plural marker "-vu" is attached to the noun stem ("birds" = "vumavu"), and the number word "two" becomes "nani" in this context (likely a plural-specific form or agreement marker). The verb "vuma" remains invariant. I need to test the indefinite article "a" with a plural noun (which the prompt says should be omitted) to confirm the rule, and test other verbs to ensure they are also invariant. I will also test if the number word "two" changes position or form when it modifies the subject versus the object.

PROBES:
1. two birds see a boat
2. two birds see two boats
3. a bird see a boat (re-test to confirm "a" is truly absent for indefinite plural)
4. a bird feed a small tree (testing a different verb)
action.label: response

probes: a bird see a boat (re-test to confirm "a" is truly absent for indefinite plural)
a bird feed a small tree (testing a different verb)

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "two birds see a boat"  =>  vumavu nani pato fovu
2. "two birds see two boats"  =>  vumavu nani patovu nani fovu
3. "a bird see a boat (re-test to confirm "a" is truly absent for indefinite plural)"  =>  MALFORMED: unexpected word 're' in the object
4. "a bird feed a small tree (testing a different verb)"  =>  MALFORMED: unexpected word 'testing' in the object
STATUS: investigation round 4 of 5 done. Probes used: 16 of 20. 1 investigation round(s) left before the exam (10 sentences).
Send your next probes now (a PROBES list; brief THOUGHTS optional).
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: My last two probes were malformed because I accidentally included explanatory text ("re-test", "testing") inside the English sentence structure instead of just the sentence itself. The system correctly rejected them. I have confirmed: SVO order, invariant verb "vuma", definite article "na" after noun, plural marker "-vu" on noun stem, and "nani" as the word for "two" (or a plural agreement marker). I need to test a few more verbs to ensure they are all invariant and check if the number word "two" changes form depending on whether it modifies the subject or object (though the previous results suggest "nani" is consistent for "two"). I will also test the verb "find" and "carry" to be thorough before the final round.

PROBES:
1. a bird find a boat
2. two birds find a boat
3. a bird carry a small stone
4. two birds carry two stones
action.label: response

probes: a bird carry a small stone

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "a bird find a boat"  =>  vuma pato fepepe
2. "two birds find a boat"  =>  vumavu nani pato fepepe
3. "a bird carry a small stone"  =>  vuma finu notu fuma
4. "two birds carry two stones"  =>  vumavu nani notuvu nani fuma

FINAL EXAM. Investigation is over. Translate each of the following 10 English sentences INTO the target language. Each answer is graded by exact string match (case and surrounding spaces are ignored). Every sentence uses only words you have already seen translated.

1. the four hunters watch four small roads
2. the six red rivers carry two hills
3. five red hunters hear a old bird
4. four farmers see two hunters
5. a hunter chase two spears
6. the three small trees chase two spears
7. two big trees chase two hills
8. the six small farmers hold four cold lakes
9. five small trees chase three hills
10. two rivers see the three farmers

Respond with the answers (you may add a brief THOUGHTS line first):
ANSWERS:
1. <translation of sentence 1>
... through 10.
action.label: context

action.content:
